data.title: Why UnitedHealth Is Down 50% In A Month And What Happens Next
data.description:
Written by Sam Kovacs

Introduction

It's not been a great month for pharmacy benefit managers (referred to as PBMs from here on).

A few days ago, President Trump said in a press conference:

We're going to cut out the middlemen and facilitate the direct sale of drugs at the most favored nation price, directly to the American citizen.

The most favored nation price means that Americans should be able to buy the drug at the cheapest price it is sold at in foreign countries.

One argument put forth is that with inflated prices in the US, it is the consumer who is footing the global bill for research and development in the pharma industry. The second argument put forth, is that this is not even the case, it is the PBMs that are extracting most of the value.

Eli Lilly (LLY) has claimed that "60% of the cost of medicine goes to middle men".

Over the past few decades, the big 3 PBMs have grown to about 80% of US prescriptions.

It's not clear to everyone what a PBM does, but basically, they are "the middleman" between pharmaceutical companies producing drugs, health insurers, and pharmacies.

They usually negotiate rebates with the manufacturer, to get the drug at a cheaper cost, but only pass through part of that rebate to the payer, pocketing the rest for itself. This has created an extractive dynamic which creates an incentive for Pharma companies to jack up prices, to then offer rebates. Everyone wins but the US consumer who foots the bill.

UnitedHealth Group (UNH), Cigna (CI), and CVS Health (CVS) all plunged following the news, as the charts below show. UNH was hit the hardest, because it was trading at the ripest valuation.

This is of particular interest to me, because as luck would have it, the only one of the 3 stocks in my portfolio is UNH, having bailed on CVS last year.

The questions become: Can Trump really "cut out the middleman"? What are the legal and regulatory paths to dismantle them? What are the implications for PBMs, and in particular for UNH?

What's the legal and regulatory route?

To cut out PBMs, or even just significantly weaken their position requires significant changes in the way the system operates.

The first path would be legislative action, as bipartisan bills have already been introduced in Congress to limit the power PBMs have over negotiations.

There is the PBM Transparency act, which seeks to prevent their ability to charge health plans more than they then pay the pharmacy for the drug (which is called spread pricing). It would also require PBMs to pass through all of their rebates to the payer, therefore eliminating a significant source of revenue.

These acts wouldn't "cut out the middleman" per se, but rather force them into a more transparent business model, reducing their moat in the process.

There have been reforms put forth at the state level as well, often more aggressive than what is being presented at the federal level. An Arkansas law passed last month prevents PBMs from owning pharmacies, which reduces the possibility for vertical integration in the industry.

So there is a clear movement that is zeroing-in on PBMs.

Trump 1.0's HHS failed to eliminate the legal safe harbor for drug rebates, which would force manufacturers to give rebates straight to the pharmacy, removing the need for PBMs. The feared knock-on effects of such a policy were that insurers would raise premiums to offset the loss of rebate income, and that therefore government spending would increase substantially as they foot the bill for Medicare premiums.

So it's not as easy as killing rebates. There needs to be cost controls elsewhere, or the US consumer ends up footing the bill in some form or another.

Trump's latest executive order, signed a few days ago, is taking the route of matching the price of "most favored nations" as I mentioned in the introduction. It gave just 30 days for the industry to align, which is not clear is even possible, and it's not clear how such a policy would be enforced.

What is clear is that there are a lot of obstacles to be overcome to truly eliminate PBMs. The likelihood is that they are weakened. Maybe Medicare will bargain directly with drug manufacturers. But what about all the private sector? A reshuffling would take colossal efforts.

Is it possible and feasible? Sure. But there is a big difference between "talking the talk" and "walking the walk".

The PBM industry has massive lobbying influence, because the major PBMs are part of massive healthcare companies.

In 2023, PBM reforms were going to go through a year-end congressional package, only to get removed at the 11th hour, revealing some behind the scenes pressure from the industry.

The presidential support would need to persist throughout the mandate, and congress would need to pass serious reforms.

However, it seems likely that sooner or later, some significant changes will be upheld.

Implications for UNH

UNH is one of the most vertically integrated healthcare stocks, being both the US's largest insurer, and the owner of one of the big 3 PBMs, which is part of its OptumRx business.

It's clear that any changes to PBMs would have a certain impact on UNH, but this hasn't been fully calculated or estimated. Instead, fear has pushed the company down a massive 54% in a month.

Someone must ask the question: is this overkill?

Earlier this year, CEO Andrew Witty did a preemptive strike, realizing that the walls were closing in on rebates. He committed OptumRx to passing through 100% of rebates to clients by 2028. His stepping down for "personal reasons" since, adds uncertainty at the worst time.

If reform happens faster, these changes might have to happen faster than planned, which is compounding uncertainty.

A total elimination of PBMs would make OptumRx a simple claims administrator, which would significantly slash its margins. Seems unlikely to me.

Another possibility would be the obligation to spin off the PBM, thus dismantling the vertical integration that has been the backbone of UNH's moat and the basis of our investment thesis. UNH would, of course, fight such a proposal tooth and nail, arguing that integration yields efficiencies and better health outcomes.

Forcing a PBM spin-off would be a far worse outcome for UNH. However, I don't believe that it is an outcome which is likely. The lobbying pressure, colossal legislative and regulatory reform necessary, opposition from Wall Street, and multi-year complications make it unlikely.

So the most likely end game in this whole debacle, in my opinion is a gutting of PBM profits from spread pricing and rebates, not a forced vertical breakup which would send the whole industry in a tailspin for years as it restructures.

The best outcome for UNH would be if it is simply required a combination of increased transparency and flat fees on spreads/rebates. I think this might be overly bullish at this point given the populist bent.

What I view as the base case, is that we move towards a near total spread pricing ban, and removal of rebate retention. Whether this happens before UNH's 2028 timeline will depend on how much of a push we see from the government.

A full structural breakup, which it seems the market is pricing with some possibility at this point, seems to me like a tail risk. Not to be ignored, but not to be priced as the base case.

Back of the envelope financials

Let's do some back-of-the-envelope calculations of the financial impact.

UNH makes $400bn in revenue, which translates into about $30bn in operating income. If that seems like not much, it is because $260bn of that $400bn collected disappears into policy benefits from its insurer arm.

OptumRx revenue is about $120bn, and estimates suggest that about 15% to 25% of its gross profit is derived from rebates and spread pricing. We can guesstimate from financial presentations that about $3bn of EBIT could be at risk.

This is a rough estimate derived from the fact that Optum is broken down into Optum Health, Optum Insight, and OptumRx, and that they collectively bring in $18bn of operating income, of which we know $4bn is derived from Optum insight.

If we take margin compression of OptumRx's 4-6% operating margins to 1-2%, there could be a direct impact of about 5% EPS at UNH.

Let's say I'm being too conservative, and it shaves off 10% of UNH's earnings.

We go from $19-20 diluted EPS to $17-18. The rest of UNH's growth engine continues to grow.

The dividend is $8.40. It's not at risk.

But UNH now yields 2.7%.

I think it might be worth increasing our position.

Even if a reshuffling of the business model would take us away from mid-teen growth down to low double-digit growth, the correction here is in my opinion, overdone. If a tail risk does come through it could go significantly lower, but I believe that at 10-12x earnings, the probability of significant harm is being totally overestimated by the market, and that this 50% drop could correct, providing a nice capital gain cushion and allowing investors to lock in an attractive yield for UNH.

There is some risk in the worst-case scenarios coming to light, but I feel the risk/reward is skewed in our favor here.

Make sure to note that you're catching a falling knife here, and there is no historical precedent in the past 30 years for UNH yielding this much.

So you want to ease in. Markets hate uncertainty, and there is plenty now. Worst-case scenarios would be further decretive to UNH, and they are possible but being overpriced at these levels IMO.

Say you're willing to build up to a 5% position in UNH, and like me, your position is currently 0.5% of the portfolio (it was about 1%, but recent performance changed that...)

This would be a reasonable time to add maybe 1% of your portfolio to the position. Over the next 3–4 quarters, you can gradually add more as more developments come to light and uncertainty reduces.

Conclusion

There are a lot of headline risks. Significant change is in the air. Worst-case scenarios are unlikely. Look at this administration's shoot for the moon and settle approach with tariffs. The endgame is not the opening game, and the market is jittery right now.

I think at $275-$280, UNH is definitely worth a position at this point. Just be cautious and don't risk everything. Small bets go a long way.

data.title: The Theoretical Problem Of L2 In Ethereum
data.description:
Introduction

Repeatedly, and we could say that it is beginning to be vox populi, I have heard or read that Ethereum has a big problem. That it is not able to properly monetise its technological infrastructure, that the protocol itself yields to other layer 2 solutions, rollups or zero knowledge proofs. In today's article we will try to unravel that point, in order to offer a vision of where Ethereum is and some of the solutions that run on its layer, Arbitrum, Optimism, Base or Polygon (PoS). We will study all these protocols from the point of view of fee generation and how they all relate to layer 1 (Ether).

Repeatedly, and we could say that it is beginning to be vox populi, I have heard or read that Ethereum has a big problem. That it is not able to properly monetise its technological infrastructure, that the protocol itself yields to other layer 2 solutions, rollups or zero knowledge proofs. In today's article we will try to unravel that point, in order to offer a vision of where Ethereum is and some of the solutions that run on its layer, Arbitrum, Optimism, Base or Polygon (PoS). We will study all these protocols from the point of view of fee generation and how they all relate to layer 1 (Ether). Before we start, we need a bit of context. Ethereum changed its consensus protocol in September 2022 from Proof Of Work (PoW) to Proof Of Stake (PoS) (The Merge). The only thing you need to know about this is that it changed the computational power by temporarily locking down the ETH assets held by the miners, now validators. If before you had to dedicate a lot of computational resources to ‘mining’, now you have to block a minimum amount of Ether to be able to validate the chain. We explain this because after this update came several (London, Dencun, Pectra...), but we will stop at the second one; Dencun.

Dencun, activated on Ethereum on 13 March 2024, marked a milestone in the evolution of the network by introducing the concept of proto-danksharding through the implementation of EIP-4844. This technical improvement has allowed rollups (such as Arbitrum, Optimism or Base) to publish their data on the core network through a new format called blobs, which is much cheaper than traditional call data. These blobs are temporary data segments (~128 kB each) that cannot be accessed by smart contracts and are automatically deleted after approximately 18 days. The fees associated with these blobs - called blob base fees - are part of a separate market from conventional gas and, like the base fee for traditional gas, are completely burned, reducing the supply of ETH. The impact was immediate: transaction fees for rollups have been reduced by up to 90%, relieving base layer congestion and reinforcing Ethereum's role as an availability and consensus layer.

Analysis

In this first graph, we show the price evolution of the price of ETH compared to the evolution of the fees generated by layer 2 or zero knowledge protocols, such as Arbitrum, Optimism, Base, Polygon (MATIC) or zkSync. We have removed from this graph the fees generated by layer 1 (ETH) for the sake of clarity. It can be clearly seen, and the spearman correlation coefficients confirm (0.74) that when there is an increase in the generation of fees from these protocols there is an expansion of the price of ETH. Even so, these fees generated do not go to the Ethereum protocol itself, but rather most of them are burned (Base Fees) and only the Priority Fee ends up in the native network, in the form of a reward to the validators that secure the chain itself. For now, we have a key factor in understanding the economics of Ether.

The green bars are weekly increases of more than 10% in the generation of these fees, which coincide with local peaks in Ethereum's own price.

In the following graph, we can see the same as in the previous one, but with a comparison of the price of the Optimism token. As mentioned above, Optimism is a layer 2 rollup that uses Ethereum's infrastructure to guarantee the same levels of security, but optimising transactional capacity and cost. Here too, the local maximums of the asset price coincide with the maximums of the fees generated by the different protocols analysed. The spearman correlation coefficient in this case is slightly lower than the previous one (0.62). The green bars, as in the previous section, signify weekly growth of more than 15% in the fees variable.

In the following graph, we can see the same as in the previous one, but with a comparison of the price of the Optimism token. As mentioned above, Optimism is a layer 2 roll-up that uses Ethereum's infrastructure to guarantee the same levels of security, but optimising transactional capacity and cost. Here too, the local maximums of the asset price coincide with the maximums of the fees generated by the different protocols analysed. The spearman correlation coefficient in this case is slightly lower than the previous one (0.62). The green bars, as in the previous section, signify weekly growth greater than 15% in the fees variable.

To answer the question, we posed at the beginning of this article, is Ethereum not monetising the ‘rent’ of its infrastructure well, and does that pose a short/medium term challenge for the network? We look at the graph below, which relates the price of Ethereum vs. the percentage of fees represented by each of the protocols analysed, including Ethereum. We show data from October 2022, as ‘The Merge’ update was implemented in September 2022. It can be seen that at some points in time, layer 2 solutions are eating into Ethereum's share of total fee generation, but this is not aligned with the price of the asset. There is no correlation. The fact that layer 2 solutions are generating more fees in percentage terms does not mean that the core network is obsolete or does not know how to monetise its technological infrastructure. Because the Ethereum protocol wins on the other hand. While it is true that it does not monetise per se the fact that others use its technological infrastructure, it does burn most of the fees generated by these protocols, which makes the main protocol deflationary if the underlying protocols (layer 2, rollups and zk) have an increase in users and/or transactions. In the end, fees are composed of two elements:

  • Base fee: fee generated by transacting on layer 2 itself, which is always burned.

  • Priority fee: fee paid for faster transaction processing. These fees are not burned and will reward validators who block their Ether to validate the chain.

Knowing this, the only fees that enter the main chain are the priority fees, but the entire base fee is burned, reducing the circulating supply of Ethereum. That is the big point of this issue. Ethereum does not directly charge those who use its infrastructure, but it burns most of the Ethereum spent by users interacting with those protocols that use its technology stack.

Conclusion

In conclusion, I firmly believe that there is no problem in the short/medium term with the issues raised here. If the reader is considering buying Ethereum, I recommend monitoring these types of variables on sites such as Artemis Crypto, Dune Analytics or native blockchain explorers such as Etherscan or Polygonscan. With the new update (Pectra) recently implemented on the network, I think it may be a good time to buy for the medium to long term. As always, beware of short-term volatility, monitor Bitcoin, which is king, and have a balanced portfolio, especially in crypto.

data.title: Everybody Loves Uber Right Now, But I'm Getting Cautious
data.description:
Uber (NYSE:UBER) has just reached its all-time high, and as usual, the market now seems less vocal about some risks that once seemed very concrete, such as Tesla (TSLA) dominating the self-driving car market (and consequently threatening Uber's market).

Since my last analysis, the stock has shown gains of 30%, and since the first analysis, 50% gains. This means that the margin of safety is lower, even though the company has delivered good results in recent quarters, which makes the prospects even better and more reliable.

Uber's thesis remains largely intact. The outlook is very positive in the long term, top-line and profitability growth is occurring, and it looks like a trend that will last, i.e., I still believe in an increase in shareholder value in the long term. The most critical change since the last analysis is in relation to the margin of safety, as the stock has shown substantial gains.

The Margin Of Safety Just Got Worse

Let's start with the margin of safety. If you check out my latest analysis, you'll see that in a model to find a proxy for fair value for Uber, I found something close to $87. But this needs to be interpreted with a few caveats. Instead of $87, using the same assumptions (which still fit perfectly), we find something closer to $90, as the table below shows.

For this to really be the fair value of Uber stock, the scenario needs to be moderate, with a top-line growing between 8% and 14%, a gradually advancing net income margin, consider a terminal growth of 3% and finally, discount this net income by a discount rate of 10% (even if Uber's WACC is below this value).

In other words, if exactly this scenario occurs (and I particularly see that even better numbers could materialize), Uber stock should produce gains of close to 10% for the shareholder if bought for around $90

There are, therefore, two main reasons why Uber's rating is a buy and not a hold (or even a sell). The first is that the assumptions implicit in this scenario are not exactly optimistic. Uber itself considers that gross earnings for the next 3 years will grow in the mid to high teens, while adj. EBITDA will show a CAGR of high 30s% to 40%. Not only that, but after these three years, Uber will still have a lot of growth to capture, either in its core through penetration into new regions or with new functionalities and maturing optionalities.

The second point is that even if this scenario is used as the standard, 10% per year is far from a bad return for a company with upside risk.

But in any case, it's worth mentioning that there's a substantial difference in the margin of safety from when the stock was around $75 to now, when it's at $90, which is why I think the company deserves a downgrade to just “buy”, since the asymmetry has become smaller and slightly less attractive.

Some Risks For The Long-Term Thesis

Now that the stock has advanced and the margin of safety has decreased, it's the best time to revisit the risks and see if they really are probable risks or risks with a limited impact, which the margin of safety can absorb and even with these potential challenges the company can still deliver a decent result for the shareholder.

Regarding AVs, I believe that this excerpt from the earnings call shows both what mitigates the risk and the potential of this trend for Uber:

“I think a lot of you probably saw the announcement of Waymo partnering up with Toyota - that’s just indicative, we think, of where AV is going, which is you’re got pure play software developers increasingly offering more sophisticated AV platforms to OEMs around the world, and a world in which 10 years from now every single new car sold comes with Level 4, Level 5 AV, we think is a terrific outcome in terms of safety for the streets, and also our platform which will allow any player, any owner of those vehicles, whether it’s financial institutions, etc. to monetize those vehicles with the highest utilization, so that they’ve got the lowest cost of capital.”

Another factor affecting both AVs and Uber's core right now is regulation. Regulation is very present in Uber's thesis worldwide, and as one or another law applies in regions, it can end up affecting Uber's revenue and margin either temporarily or indefinitely. A recent example was House Bill 1291, which caused Uber to mention that it might even “shut down operations” in Colorado.

These bills in the US and around the world should not only be monitored when such news comes out, but are also a factor that raises the degree of uncertainty of the thesis and makes it more risky.

Another important point is the take rate and competition. If you search for reports, there are already complaints about Uber's take rate. In mobility, if you look at revenue/gross bookings, it's around 30%. The take rate isn't exactly 30% and can vary, and this revenue doesn't take into account only Uber's “traditional” rides. But this account is enough to suggest that this take rate is already close to a natural limit, at least considering human drivers. One of the factors that makes me think this is competition. Increasing this take rate even more could not only generate driver churn, but also encourage competition from similar apps such as Lyft (LYFT) or local apps in certain regions.

All this must be considered. Even if we believe that Uber at current prices can generate a total return of around 10% per year and is a stock with a still favorable level of asymmetry, this return is not without risk. In fact, although Uber is a very interesting company with a lot of potential in different markets, it is far from being at the same level of quality as companies like Costco (COST) or Apple (AAPL), which have such broad moats and such a profitable business model that prospects are very likely.

In the Long Term, The Thesis Remains Intact

The chart below illustrates how Uber's thesis remains intact. Quarter by quarter, the company has made significant operational progress, which is reflected in the financials. The free cash flow of $7.8 billion in the last 12 months is already very interesting, given that the current market cap is less than $200 billion, giving us a multiple of around 24x, but what is most impressive is the pace at which it is advancing.

Another image that sums up the thesis is this one:

Uber is a leader in several regions, but in a market that is growing and has very positive prospects. As I said, there is competition and other risks, but it is still a market with good prospects related to consumer preference, digitalization trends, and the like. Momentum is also very positive, with gross bookings advancing by almost 20% in Q1.

The third chart is also very important. The thesis not only combines accelerated operational growth that converts into gross bookings and revenue (as well as tons of opportunities that can accelerate the top-line in the long term), but also margin expansion. With scale and an increasingly optimized operation, the margin gain looks very clear in the long term, and with it, a substantial increase in free cash flow.

In other words, Uber combines plenty of growth potential through a quality company in a promising market with a very reasonable valuation (which should continue to improve as the company unlocks shareholder value).

The Bottom Line

It may seem that this analysis has become too bearish. But in fact, I remain bullish on Uber's thesis. There is a lot of potential for the company to exploit, and its momentum remains great, as Q1 with high YoY growth in gross booking shows, and this trend becomes even better when combined with margin expansion.

On the other hand, the higher the stock rises, the more necessary it becomes to ensure that the margin of safety remains attractive, and that the risks are manageable, or at least the risk-return ratio remains good.

That said, I believe that the risk-reward is still good, but not as great as it was before, and so I'm downgrading Uber stock to “buy”.

data.title: Vantage Drilling: Transformation Continues But Prospects Remain Difficult To Assess - Hold
data.description:
Note:

Vantage Drilling International Ltd. or "Vantage Drilling" (VTDRF) has been discussed by me previously, so investors should view this article as an update to my earlier coverage of the company.

In recent quarters, Dubai-based offshore driller Vantage Drilling has been executing on a number of strategic transactions targeted at transforming the company from an offshore driller into a provider of rig management services:

Earlier this week, Vantage Drilling reported Q1 2025 results which were reflective of recent rig sales and persistent idle time for the 6th generation drillship Platinum Explorer.

As a result, revenues decreased by almost 50% on a sequential basis and adjusted EBITDA turned negative. The company recorded negative free cash flow of $12.7 million and finished the quarter with $73.1 million in unrestricted cash as well as $65.1 million in debt.

That said, backlog more than tripled to $211.2 million, mostly due to the recent execution of a 10-year management agreement for the 7th generation drillship Tungsten Explorer which will be sold to a joint venture between the company (25%) and TotalEnergies (75%) later this year. The agreement includes a five-year extension option.

The sale will result in cash proceeds of $198.8 million, thus increasing the company's pro forma net cash position from $8 million to $206.5 million. While Vantage Drilling has not yet decided upon the use of proceeds, a sizable one-time dividend might be in the cards.

Assuming a $100 million special dividend, common shareholders would receive approximately $7.50 per share:

Last month, Vantage Drilling received a conditional letter of award for the Platinum Explorer with a validity period of 90 days. However, contract terms don't look great:

The contract value is circa $80M with an anticipated 260 days required to render the campaign, inclusive of mobilization time, paid-for contract preparation time and demobilization time. A material portion of the contract value, covering mobilisation and paid-for contract preparation, is reimbursed based on the cost incurred and a limited margin amount.

The company also signed a marketing agreement with Eldorado Drilling AS for the 7th generation drillship Dorado:

On February 4, 2025, the Company entered into a marketing agreement with Eldorado Drilling AS to market the Seventh Generation Dorado Drillship for drilling opportunities in various locations in Africa, the Mediterranean, Asia and Australasia.

Following the proposed sale of the Tungsten Explorer to the new joint venture with TotalEnergies, Vantage Drilling's remaining rig assets will consist of the Platinum Explorer and its 25% stake in the Tungsten Explorer.

Since my last update on the company, estimated net asset value ("NAV") per share hasn't really changed:

That said, I have lowered my value assumption for the 6th generation drillship Platinum Explorer by 30% due to increased uncertainties resulting from escalating trade tensions and increased OPEC+ supply levels. However, this was offset by much lower cash consumption than anticipated by me in January.

While management hasn't stated plans for a potential sale of the Platinum Explorer, I wouldn't be surprised to see the company divesting its last fully owned rig at some point going forward. However, finding a buyer would be difficult in the current market environment.

Going forward, Vantage Drilling will mostly serve as a rig management platform, with the company actively looking to expand its managed fleet.

With less than 2,000 shares traded on the U.S. Pink Sheets over the past six months, I have used the price of the company's shares in Oslo as the basis for my NAV discount calculation:

Despite an almost 30% discount to estimated NAV and a potential special dividend payment later this year, a number of issues are keeping me on the sidelines:

  • Very illiquid trading in the company's shares
  • Uncertainties regarding the earnings potential of the new asset-light business model
  • Net asset value and earnings upside remain highly dependent on assumptions regarding the Platinum Explorer

Consequently, I am reiterating my "Hold" rating on the company's shares.

Bottom Line

Vantage Drilling's first quarter 2025 results reflected recent asset sales and persistent idle time for the drillship Platinum Explorer.

The company's transformation into a rig management services provider continues, but it will likely take another couple of quarters until the earnings potential of the new business model becomes fully assessable.

Even with prospects for a large special dividend in the second half of the year, I prefer to remain on the sidelines as a result of the above-discussed issues.

Consequently, I am reiterating my "Hold" rating on Vantage Drilling's shares.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Vital Energy: About Those Acquisition Benefits
data.description:
The last article on Vital Energy (VTLE) mentioned that acquisitions have benefits. Those benefits are finally starting to be apparent to investors with the latest quarterly report. There are likely more benefits on the way from a combination of good acreage and continuing technology advances that keep lowering operating costs (or at least keep those costs rising long-term at less than the rate of inflation).

Dallas Federal Reserve

The Oil & Gas Journal summarized the industry replies to the Dallas Federal Reserve study made public about "costs". This is an area that leaves many investors confused, so let's start with what might be called a "reasonable standard" that the Dallas Federal Reserve concluded as a result of the survey.

"Not so fast, respondents to the Dallas Fed poll said: On the opex question, their responses averaged $41/bbl (up from $39 a year ago) and ranged from $26 in the Eagle Ford to $45 in parts of the Permian outside the Delaware and Midland basins.

But when it comes to justifying investments in new wells, respondents said they need at least $61 (in the Midland) to $70 in the 'other' parts of the Permian. The average price they need to profitably drill a new well is $65, up from $64 in early 2024. Firms producing at least 10,000 b/d have a cost advantage: Their leaders said they can drill at a WTI prices of $61/bbl while smaller producers said they need $66/bbl to expand."

Keep in mind that not only are the well costs included in those final figures, but also company costs like administrative and interest. It is one thing to drill a new well that adds to cash flow and drops the corporate breakeven point. It is a whole different story that is looking at a comprehensive company situation to be able to make a profit and grow the company in a good environment.

Also note that the Delaware and Midland Basins are implied to be low-cost basins. That is where this company made its recent acquisitions. It acquired acreage from high to very high-cost operators and then set about reducing those costs. The progress is noted below.

The current environment is not very good because many managements do not know where the price of oil will be. Therefore, budgets are being cut until the uncertainty goes away. But that has later consequences for the price of oil because demand generally grows over time whether or not there is a recession in the time period reviewed.

The Company's Breakeven Position

Shown below is the company's breakeven position compared to the industry survey shown above.

The reply to a question answered by Jason Pigott during the first quarter conference call is shown below:

"Yes. The number I quoted was actually our corporate breakevens. Our well breakevens are below that or on average. Again, we're continuing to high grade the portfolio, but we're at $57 today. And again, if you -- if costs or service costs continue to come down in that 10% range, that would push it down to $53. And then again, great work that Katie is doing on LOE, can push it down further and then potential G&A reductions and things like that push it down even further. So, we could be knocking on $50 by the end of next year or through next year."

He further went on to note that the slide in the corporate presentation is using the corporate breakeven point for wells drilled. That is different from the well breakeven point which was noted to be significantly lower.

Basically, the company purchased acreage from a high-cost operator with the idea that the acreage could be operated with lower costs. The company already has lower costs than the Dallas Federal Reserve survey shown above. Management is clearly going for still lower corporate costs even though that progress is not budgeted.

That should mean that if commodity prices continue to weaken, there is a goal to remain cash flow positive "no matter what" as long as the decline is not so bad as to make the industry shrink significantly for a longer period of time than many are forecasting (or it is a steeper decline than many thought would happen).

You had better believe that something like this far overshadows the details of the first quarter report.

Debt Repayment

There was an unexpectedly large debt repayment in the first quarter.

The strategy is to repay the debt from the acquisition while maintaining that higher quality production (and hopefully improving the situation along the way).

The debt repayment was unexpectedly large. Whether that happens again depends upon the interaction between current commodity prices combined with the operational progress made throughout the fiscal year.

This is a very low-visibility industry. We could have a price decline that causes the industry to shrink (while setting the stage for sky-high prices in the future again). Here, the acreage is good enough for management to maintain positive cash flow as long as the commodity prices do not move faster than the cost progress made by management.

New Well Design

Slowly the company breakeven point for new wells drilled is coming down as management tries new designs for wells. At the rate these new designs keep appearing, it would appear that there is a lot of future potential that is not in the guidance because the future can vary quite a bit. But it is also clear that progress will continue to be made.

The last design implemented (company-wide) is shown on the upper right-hand side of the slide. It affects roughly 14% of the inventory. Now, the company has drilled yet another new well design shown on the lower lefthand side of the slide. It has yet to be determined the potential for this design on future costs.

Impairment Charge

There was a valuation allowance for the full-cost ceiling calculation. GAAP allows a number of variations in this calculation. However, it is important to note that a number of the factors are historical in nature. Therefore, some of the gains reported by the company from new well designs will not be included in the calculation until there is sufficient history.

The 10-Q notes that second quarter valuation allowance could be in the $200 million range. Some countries like Canada allow this impairment to be recovered as commodity prices cyclically recover because it becomes obvious that the costs are reasonable and therefore can be put back in a rising ceiling calculation. The United States, however, makes no provision for the cyclical recovery of commodity prices (which allows the reversal of the impairment charge).

Management stated the following in the earnings press release about the situation:

"The impairment was the result of the full cost ceiling limitation, driven in part by the decline in the trailing 12-month oil price calculation, and excludes the value of $145.9 million for the Company's commodity derivative positions and only includes the 185 proved undeveloped locations in the Company's reserve report out of approximately 925 inventory locations."

Management (and the reserve report) is not going to report to shareholders a lot more locations if the company cannot make money with those locations. But the full-cost calculation is only concerned with what "is or was" profitable given the input values at the current time. A new design is too speculative for this calculation until its use is proven. The fact that those input values are highly likely to increase as the Dallas Federal Reserve strongly implies in the Oil & Gas Journal article quote shown above is largely irrelevant to the calculation.

That makes the charge largely irrelevant unless it can be demonstrated that cash flow is materially impaired even with a commodity price recovery. Such events are extremely rare. An example was back in 2016 before we knew that the days of $90 (and above) WTI were largely over rather than commonplace. Cash flows as a result were never going to be like they were before, and the industry growth was about to slow materially. That does not appear to be the case now.

Summary

Vital Energy has made some solid progress with the recent acquisitions that were made. Cost progress has already resulted in a better structure that management will likely continue to try to improve going forward. While this turnaround has taken more time than anticipated, the days of large negative cash flows and subsequent asset sales to clean up the balance sheet are largely over. The new debt market accepted, and stock market viable free cash flow model is developing. That reinforces the strong buy recommendation even if this turnaround took longer than others I follow.

I would hold this stock until the story changes materially.

Risks

Despite the fact that technological advances have been happening for ages. There is no way to forecast the effect of technological advances or when they will occur. Technology advances could stop tomorrow (however unlikely that is) which may considerably slow or stop the company cost progress.

The article was written with the idea that the current threat of tariffs and trade wars will be replaced by something that is more permanent and constructive than the current situation. There is no assurance that will happen.

Similarly, any acquisition can fail at any time to meet company expectations. In this case, it is unlikely that all the acquisitions would do that. But it could happen to one or two as the upstream business is just loaded with surprises.

Any upstream business is subject to the low visibility and volatility of future commodity prices. A severe and sustained downturn in those prices could materially change the company's outlook.

A loss of key personnel could materially set back the company.

data.title: Third Coast Bancshares: Still Bullish Despite Its Recent Plunge
data.description:
Back in early February of this year, one financial firm that I found to be worthy of a ‘buy’ rating was Third Coast Bancshares (NASDAQ:TCBX). If you haven't heard of the company before, that's alright. It's a rather small player with a market capitalization right now of $428.4 million. However, it was larger in February. You see, back then, I lauded the company for its rapid growth and deposits and loans, as well as its successful efforts to expand in major markets in Texas, such as Houston, Austin, and the Dallas-Fort Worth area. At the end of the day, asset quality proved high enough and shares were priced cheap enough relative to earnings to justify a solid ‘buy’ rating. But since then, shares have pulled back, falling 17% at a time when the S&P 500 is down 3.9%.

At first glance, you might think that something was wrong. But the fact of the matter is that the company continues to achieve growth from an income statement perspective. Net interest margin is rising, and debt remains low. Unfortunately, there was a slight decline in deposits during this time. But outside of that, the picture for the business looks robust. And with management forecasting loan growth this year, I believe that maintaining a ‘buy’ rating is the right choice. And honestly, if shares fall much further from here, I think that upgrading the company to a ‘strong buy’ might not be too far off.

A bank worth considering

Perhaps the best place to start off with Third Coast Bancshares would be to cover the most recent quarterly data, which covers the first quarter of 2025. On the balance sheet side of things, the picture was a bit mixed. As an example, deposits for the institution came in at $4.25 billion. This represents a small drop from the $4.31 billion the company had at the end of 2024. But as you can tell by looking at the chart above, the company has a solid track record of achieving consistent growth.

This drop for the company was primarily caused by a decline in non-interest-bearing demand accounts. Those deposits fell from $602.1 million to $448.5 million. In its first-quarter earnings call, the company's management team mentioned that this is a typical seasonal decline in deposits. But when I looked at the deposit trends for the last three fiscal years, I could not see such a seasonal drop from the final quarter of the previous year. This is not to say that management is wrong or lying. It could just be that, in prior years, organic and acquisition-fueled growth for the institution more than offset organic deposit declines. And for this year, that was not the case.

When it comes to the firm’s loan portfolio, we saw a change from $3.93 billion in 2024 to $3.95 billion in the first quarter of this year. I know that's not a large change. However, management did say that they expect total loan growth this year to be around $325 million. And subsequent to the end of the first quarter, in April, the company achieved $50 million in loan expansion. The largest chunk of exposure that the institution has when it comes to its loans would be commercial and industrial loans. These accounted for $1.61 billion, or 40.3%, of the company's gross loan portfolio for the quarter. Construction, development, and other related loans came in second at $846.2 million. This was followed by non-farm, non-residential, non-owner-occupied real estate, as well as non-farm, non-residential, owner-occupied space.

One thing that I would like to touch on regarding its loans is that the picture has improved when it comes to those that are classified as non-accrual. In the most recent quarter, the company had $17.1 million of non-accrual loans. That compares to the $26.8 million reported for the end of 2024. When talking about the loans that are past due, there was a slight worsening, but it was very small, from $34.8 million to $34.9 million.

There are, of course, other aspects of the balance sheet that deserve attention. One example would be securities. During the quarter, the company had $397.4 million worth. That's up from the $384 million reported at the end of last year. Cash and cash equivalents did drop from $421.6 million to $329.7 million. But it appears to me that management has just shifted that cash to more productive things like securities and loans. Meanwhile, debt remained almost unchanged, inching up ever so slightly from $111.6 million to $111.7 million.

When it comes to the income statement, we have seen some improvements also. Net interest income popped from $36.5 million in the first quarter of 2024 to $42.3 million in the first quarter of this year. The change in the composition of the company's balance sheet certainly contributed to this. However, the firm also enjoyed a rise in its net interest margin from 3.60% to 3.80%. Part of this improvement in its net interest income came from the fact that its provision for credit losses contracted from $1.6 million last year to only $0.5 million this year.

The company does generate some revenue from non-interest activities. But this is a very small amount. During the quarter, non-interest income was only $3.1 million. That was up from a $2.3 million reported the previous year, with most of that change attributable to a jump in service charges and fees from $1.5 million to $2.3 million. This improvement, combined with the aforementioned rise in net interest income, pushed the company's overall net profits up from $9.2 million to $12.4 million.

Seeing as how it is so early in 2025, I think it would be best to value the company using historical results from last year instead. Doing this, as shown in the chart above, we get a price to earnings multiple for the institution of 10. That is precisely at the high end of the range that I like to see in this industry. Relative to similar companies, the stock looks cheap, though. In the same chart, we can see that Third Coast Bancshares ended up being cheaper than the five institutions I compared it to. Unfortunately, the tables turn a little bit when it comes to the price to book and price to tangible book multiples. In the chart below, you can see precisely what I mean. On a price-to-book basis, Third Coast Bancshares ended up being the most expensive of the group. And on a price-to-tangible-book basis, we can see that four of the five institutions shown in the chart are cheaper than it is. The good news here is that while the company might be pricey compared to similar firms, it's trading at only slightly above book value and tangible book value. I would say that this is not awful by any means.

On top of being attractively priced and continuing to grow, Third Coast Bancshares also boasts high asset quality. In the chart below, you can see how its return on assets compares to the same five banks I stacked it up against already. The reading of 1.17% is above the 1% minimum that I typically like to see in this space. It's also superior to four of the five firms. In the subsequent chart, you can see the same thing regarding its return on equity. With a reading of 12.41%, Third Coast Bancshares is above the 10% minimum that I prefer to see, and it is above all but one of the companies I stacked it up against.

One other thing that I would like to touch on briefly is an announcement that the company made last month. According to management, on April 1st, the enterprise announced a securitization of a $200 million revolving commercial real estate loan that it originated. In essence, the enterprise made this loan to a private real estate investment firm and then packaged and sold off pieces of it to a special-purpose vehicle that then used that loan as collateral to issue asset-backed notes.

This might seem odd, but it makes a lot of sense when you consider that, under current banking regulations, certain types of loans like construction loans count in a more negative way against a bank's capital ratios. In order to benefit from the issuance of the loan while also reducing this risk, the company engaged in this transaction and essentially sold off pieces of it. This gives the company the benefit of landing the loan, while simultaneously diversifying its risk away from it. It's unclear what the full impact of this move will have on the institution. But I do like to see management making interesting moves that help to grow the bank.

Takeaway

From a purely fundamental perspective, I must say that I remain optimistic about Third Coast Bancshares. The company seems to be doing well in most respects. Shares are attractively priced, and asset quality remains high. With management expecting further growth in loans this year, and the company having a presence in certain key markets that I consider to be attractive, I have no reason not to rate it a ‘buy’.

data.title: Inter & Co Q1: Lower Risk Products Deliver Results
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I recommend buying shares of Inter & Co (INTR) after the release of the Q1 2025 results. This article is a continuation of my coverage initiation article published on April 20, 2024, with the same recommendation.

In this article, I will provide a new briefing on the company's history, but the main focus is a detailed analysis of the company's published results and linking them to my investment thesis.

Corporate Profile

Inter & Co was created in 1994 under the name Intermedium Financeira, and at the time, it belonged to MRV, a construction company listed on the Brazilian stock exchange B3 (OTCPK:BOLSY).

In 2015, now called Banco Intermedium, the company grew significantly alongside Nu (NU), offering disruptive products and services in the traditional and bureaucratic Brazilian banking market.

Today, the company is a digital bank that is experiencing strong growth. The number of customers has surpassed 37 million, gross revenues exceed BRL 3 billion ($559 million), and the credit portfolio already exceeds BRL 42 billion ($7.44 billion).

Review of Inter & Co's Results

Inter & Co released its Q1 results on May 12, and as we can see below, earnings per share came in within market expectations, and shares rose 5.2% on the day.

Below, I will provide a detailed description of the company's results, with critical analysis and perspectives. The company is Brazilian, and although it is listed on the Nasdaq, it publishes its results in Brazilian currency. Therefore, I will convert the results using the exchange rate 1 USD = 5.72 BRL, as this was the exchange rate on the last day of the quarter.

Credit Portfolio

Inter's credit portfolio rose 5% q/q and 21% y/y to BRL 37.4 billion ($6.54 billion). Highlights include the FGTS guaranteed loan, which grew 43% y/y, and home equity, which grew 45% y/y, as we can see below.

The bank will continue to focus on products with risk-adjusted returns. In my view, the private payroll loan product also seems like a good future driver, due to the bank's scalable and digital business model.

Cost of Credit and NPL

Here, we had another great result, in my opinion. NPL>90 days fell 15 bps q/q and 76 bps y/y to 4.1%, while short-term NPL rose 40 bps q/q to 3.8%, however, short-term NPL was impacted by seasonal effects, as occurred in the results of Itaú (ITUB) and Bradesco (BBD).

Credit quality is healthy, and the improvement trajectory should continue, in my view. The bank has shown a lot of pragmatism over the last few quarters and should focus on quality.

Revenues

The company's net interest income rose 8.3% q/q and 37.3% y/y to BRL 1.36 billion ($238 million). The reasons for this increase were a better product mix, asset repricing, and customer segmentation.

In my view, net interest income should remain strong due to new allocations and a better product mix. Next, we will discuss expenses before analyzing the net profit from the operation.

Expenses

The company's expenses changed -1% q/q and +32% y/y to BRL 831 million ($145 million). It is worth noting that the increase was lower than the growth in interest income, additionally, these were investments that I consider strategic, such as technology and marketing.

The efficiency gain trends should continue. Additionally, the business model is very interesting, as the increase in the customer base will help dilute costs and expenses. It is worth noting that the number of customers increased by 1.6 million in just one quarter to 37.7 million customers.

Net Income

The growth in the credit portfolio, net interest income, and expense control had an effect on the bottom line. Net income increased 4.1% q/q and 56.8% y/y to BRL 287 million ($50.2 million), while ROE rose to 12.8%.

Every quarter I see that the bank is closer to achieving its 60/30/30 plan, 60 million customers, 30% efficiency, and 30% ROE by 2027. My assessment is that the results were good, and now let's talk about valuation.

Valuation

Inter & Co has a very different business model from any other bank, so we will use the P/E multiple based on the company's own history. Let's look at the history below.

The company has traded between 8x and 17x earnings over the past year, with an average of 12.5x earnings. The company is currently trading at 10.8x earnings, a conservative upside of 15.7%, which supports the buy recommendation.

Additionally, Seeking Alpha's powerful Quant tools recommend a strong buy on the stock, which makes me even more confident in my recommendation.

Potential Threats To The Bullish Thesis

As I mentioned in the credit portfolio segment, one of the pillars of the future thesis is anchored in the private payroll loan product. If expectations for this new product are frustrated, this could mean a drop in shares.

Another risk is that one of the contributors to the growth in net interest income was the migration of fee income to this segment. This was due to regulatory changes.

If the bank is unable to maintain the strong results from this segment in the future, expectations could again be disappointed, and the share price could fall.

The Bottom Line

Inter & Co delivered strong results across all segments. The strategy of focusing on lower-risk products has delivered results and is very welcome in a macroeconomic environment as complex as Brazil's.

Revenues and credit portfolio have expanded healthily, and long-term delinquency is on a downward trajectory. Additionally, the valuation indicates a conservative upside of 15.7%.

Based on this analysis, I reiterate my recommendation to buy Inter & Co shares. In my view, the digital bank is firmly pursuing its goal of 60 million customers, 30% efficiency, and 30% ROE by 2027.

data.title: Oracle: Cloud Inflection Priced In; Initiate At Neutral
data.description:
Summary

We initiate coverage on Oracle Corporation (NYSE:ORCL) with Neutral and $145 PT. Oracle Corp. offers a suite of cloud, software, and infrastructure products globally to the enterprise. Our Neutral is based on our near-term estimates of EPS (Dil): $5.96 in FY25E (+7.2% y/y vs. Street $5.97), before accelerating to $7.25 in FY26E (+21.6% y/y vs Street $6.65), driven by easing supply constraints and the conversion of ORCL's $130B remaining performance obligations to cloud revenues.

We value ORCL based on 30.0x FY26E PE, which is a factor of 25% cloud rev growth, AI leverage in R&D ($8.9B) and Capex ($12.1B), and underappreciated spring-loaded margin expansion in FY26, while factoring in ORCL's ongoing margin deleverage in the current quarter from elevated cloud infrastructure buildout. Management's mid-teens rev guide and Project Stargate give visibility to the accelerating earnings, while supply constraints and outsized capex are drags in the near-term. Our $145 PT, implies limited TSR as we feel the spring-loaded FY26 inflection is largely baked in at current levels. We view this as balanced risk/reward and rate ORCL Neutral.

Executing for a Big FY26

We model FY25E EPS (Dil) of $5.96 (+7.2% Y/Y), slightly below Street consensus ($5.97, +11.4%) and an above-consensus FY26E of $7.25 (+21.6% Y/Y vs. Street $6.65, +11.4%). The near term conservatism is driven by guidance and modest EPS misses over Q2 and 3, as well as significant near term capex needed to support high velocity cloud deployments (which constrains profitability near term). Critically, management has indicated that supply constraints continue to limit OCI revenue recognition and margin leverage, with full impact of unprecedented RPO backlog ($130B) and data center buildout only flowing through in FY26.

Source : FMP FY25 FY26
Estimate # Analysts Estimate # Analysts
Revenue $57.08B 21 $65.09B 27
EBITDA $24.31B 21 $27.72B 27
Net Income $16.86B 21 $18.85B 27
EPS $5.97 24 $6.65 21
SG&A Expenses $12.25B 21 $13.96B 27

In contrast, the bull case for FY26 is supported by multiple high-visibility catalysts: management's explicit guidance for 15-16% revenue growth, acceleration in OCI (as it could easily exceed FY25 growth rate of 50%+), rapid RPO conversion as supply grid expands, and commercializing transformational AI initiatives (Project Stargate) that is not fully reflected in RPO or Street models. We believe consensus underestimates the magnitude and velocity of these fundamental changes, and the margin leverage they result in, giving us conviction that our above-consensus FY26 EPS view is best positioned to capture Oracle's spring-loaded growth opportunity.

Cloud Earnings Inflection

The earnings inflection to FY26 is not a mere hope but is backed by unprecedented forward RPO visibility, which in turn is a remarkable event in the history of enterprise tech: RPO has exploded to $130B (63% YoY growth) on the back of $48B+ of net new RPO signed in one quarter, of which cloud RPO grew 90%+. This is no mere pipeline: Management expects it to drive 15% rev growth in FY26, with an even steeper slope for cloud infra as new capacity comes online and supply constraints ease.

And the inflection is not even fully loaded yet: the Stargate AI project (potential $100B catalyst) is not in the numbers and is set to turbocharge the reacceleration off the starting gate. The scale of the AI backlog and the structural increase in RPO from longer and larger cloud deals create a rare springboard for EPS growth and margin expansion as capital intensity normalizes. We see the combination of these factors — record bookings, unprecedented revenue backlog, and untapped AI catalysts — as making the FY26 reacceleration both highly visible and structurally supported: a very rare combination in large-cap tech.

Valuation

Our $145 PT based on 30.0x fwd P/E multiple to our FY26E EPS of $4.84 implies -10% downside, supporting our Neutral Rating. We believe the multiple incorporates the accelerating momentum that Oracle is seeing in Cloud, backed by 25% Cloud Rev growth and $130B of RPO, and the inflection in FY26 on the earnings side as the heavy investment in AI and Cloud infrastructure should be converted into earnings power. Our PT multiple is at the 82nd percentile against ORCL's 10-year history (median: 26.6x), which we view as justified given management's explicit FY26 guidance for mid-teens rev growth and our above-consensus 21.6% EPS growth expectation for FY26 as supply chain pressures ease.

Relative to peers, ORCL still trades at a significant discount (80th percentile, 38.0x current vs 100.6x peer median) despite consensus still discounting the pace at which ORCL's massive RPO backlog can convert to earnings power once the platform constraints ease. Also, while Street focus is on margin pressures from outsized spend, we think the scale and deliberate timing of capital deployment sets up for a margin snap-back with the cadence of cloud profitability still underappreciated. Overall, we think this strikes a fair balance between the short-term operational challenges vs the spring-loaded earnings power, resulting in a fairly priced risk/reward.

Risks to Our Neutral

The key risk to our Neutral rating on ORCL is upside not fully appreciated from AI cloud strategy driving faster RPO conversion or margin expansion than we model. With aggressive doubling of capex to ramp data center capacity, ORCL could unlock a wave of OCI / multi-cloud SaaS revenue ahead of our model—especially if Project Stargate ramps faster or multi-cloud partnerships drive incremental high-margin growth not yet fully credited by the Street. Also, strategic sunset of legacy GRC suites in favor of Oracle Risk Management Cloud could accelerate SaaS migration & cross-sell dynamics, compressing cloud adoption cycles and driving operating leverage beyond Street expectations.

Finally, our thesis could be disrupted if sustained AI-driven demand results in positive earnings revisions, especially if macro environment remains supportive of enterprise IT spend and ORCL's license/support base proves more resilient than the Street expects (i.e. faster RPO recognition and operating margin snap-back). We are watching quarterly cloud backlog realization rates, OCI revenue inflections, and signs of enterprise customers accelerating adoption or achieving faster compliance transitions as potential indicators of upside risk to our current thesis.

Conclusion

We feel Oracle's compelling FY26 earnings inflection story (with a massive $130B cloud backlog) is now baked in at the current share price, which leaves us Neutral. Our thesis: there's material upside to our +DD EPS growth and margin expansion view as supply chain looseness fades, but the risk/reward feels balanced at our $145 target, which bakes in a lot of the story. We acknowledge upside surprises are possible if OCI adoption accelerates or Project Stargate outperforms, but near-term headwinds from high capex and the conversion of RPO leave us without conviction at current levels. In summary, we suggest patience: smart money should wait for quarter-to-quarter confirmation of cloud execution, backlog conversion, and the trajectory of OCI margins to see if the spring-loaded thesis is starting to materialize and is being mispriced. At these levels, we'd wait for an entry point or if fundamentals start to exceed even our optimistic out-year scenario before making a strong move.

data.title: Flower Foods: Wait For The Turnaround
data.description:
Flowers Foods shares (NYSE:FLO) have been on a steady downward trend since reaching an all-time high in November 2022. The shares are now down more than 50%.

The company manufactures and distributes packaged baked goods under brands such as Nature's Own, Dave's Killer Bread, Canyon Bakehouse, and Tastykake.

Investment thesis

With an adjusted P/E ratio of 13.2, Flowers Food currently appears to be attractively valued, especially when we look at historical multiples, which were often around 19.

However, Flowers Food has a growth problem that affects both its top and bottom lines. In the current market environment, I see little opportunity for Flowers Foods to regain its growth momentum. I also take a rather critical view of the expensive acquisition of Simple Mills for $795 million. The deal was probably only worthwhile for the previous owner of Simple Mills, but less so for Flowers Foods and its shareholders.

The dividend yield of 5.7% may be interesting for income investors, but given the high payout ratio and little room for further increases, I don't view the yield as a compelling buy argument.

Furthermore, I do not see any significant upside potential at present. The downward trend in the share price is very stable, and there is no catalyst for a sudden reversal. In such an environment, a purchase is not compelling. Investors looking for dividend yields should add the stock to their watch list and keep an eye on it. In my opinion, it makes more sense to wait for a turnaround than to invest in a business with little growth and a less-than-optimal product mix.

Growth momentum and outlook are not good

Flowers Foods is struggling with top-line and bottom-line growth. Adjusted EPS has been stagnating since 2020, and revenue growth has also virtually ground to a halt over the past two years.

Measured against the targets set in 2019 of net sales growth between 1% and 2% and adjusted EPS growth between 7% and 9%, the company is therefore quite a long way off. YoY, sales growth was only 0.25%. Flowers Foods was only able to increase its adjusted diluted EPS by $0.08 to $1.28 in 2024.

Flowers Foods is now attempting to generate inorganic growth through acquisitions. In January, the company announced that it would acquire Simple Mills, a producer of bars, cookies, and crackers, for $795 million and integrate it as an independent brand in the Flowers Foods portfolio. Simple Mills' most recent revenue was $240 million, growing at an impressive average rate of 28% per year over the last five years. Most recently, growth momentum slowed slightly to 14%, although the previous year's figure of 40% was particularly high.

With the acquisition of Simple Mills, Flowers Foods will generate inorganic growth of 5% on company-wide revenue of $5 billion. Without Simple Mills, management expects growth of only 1.5% to 3% for 2025. Conversely, the price for Simple Mills is quite high, at three times last year's revenue. Flowers Foods cannot really afford such an acquisition, as even before the takeover, it had interest-bearing debt of over $1 billion, offset by cash and cash equivalents of only $5 million. Since the Simple Mills acquisition is to be financed solely by debt, total debt is expected to rise to around $1.9 billion. Furthermore (and I credit Flowers Foods for its transparency), Simple Mills' adjusted diluted EPS will be negative and will weigh on earnings by between $0.07 and $0.04.

I take a rather critical view of the acquisition. It is difficult to justify such a premium price for a cracker and cookie manufacturer. In my experience, such brands often go through a hype phase and disappear again quite quickly. The Simple Mills story also shows how low the barriers to market entry are. Simple Mills was literally founded by Katlin Smith in 2012 in her own kitchen with start-up capital of ‘only’ $70,000. Let's take this perspective: As an investor, I would ask myself why Flowers Foods did not come up with the idea of producing and marketing such products itself. With the $795 million acquisition, the company could have launched 100 similar ventures, considering Katlin Smith's start-up capital.

One last word on market share: Flowers Foods is increasingly losing market share. The trend worsened significantly in Q4, leading to a loss of almost one percentage point in the bread segment. Although Q4 is particularly weak (presumably because consumers turn to other manufacturers during the holidays), the sudden drop in the cake segment in particular indicates that this is more of a structural than a seasonal problem.

Update: Q1 numbers are disappointing

The Q1 results for 2025 were also disappointing. Adjusted diluted EPS fell by $0.03 to $0.35. Revenue decreased by 1.4% to $1.554 billion. Inorganic revenue growth from Simple Mills was completely offset by pricing/mix and volume declines.

In addition, Flowers Foods had to lower its forecast for FY 2025. The company now expects revenue to grow only 3.8% to 5.7% instead of 5.9% to 7.5%. At least Simple Mills is driving sales here because, excluding the Simple Mills acquisition, net sales growth is expected to be -0.5% to 1.3% instead of the previous 1.5% to 3.0% growth. But here, too, my above fears are proving to be true because Simple Mills is growing less strongly than originally thought. Management expects Simple Mills to contribute $218 million to $225 million to net sales, compared to prior guidance of $223 million to $230 million. An additional problem is that the forecast for FY 2025 covers 53 weeks. On a 52-week basis, the result would therefore be even worse.

What investors get for their money

Analysts' outlook for the coming years is rather poor. They expect EPS to fall by 9% in FY 2025. Analysts do not anticipate any noticeable improvement in the following years, 2026 and 2027. I expect expectations to deteriorate even further, especially after the disappointing Q1 figures.

Of course, with a forward P/E ratio of 14.51, Flowers Foods appears cheap, especially in a historical context, considering the 5y P/E ratio median north of 25.

Conversely, what is the benefit of a low multiple when the following aspects are taken into account?

The fourth quarter in particular saw a deterioration in market share. Flowers Foods may have to offer higher discounts to combat the loss of market share. This would then have a negative impact on the already low net margin of 4.8%. In addition, the white bread and sweet baked goods categories of Flowers Foods' core business do not fit in with the nutrition-conscious trend that is set to continue in the coming years. The company is therefore struggling with structural headwinds. For a company like this, I do not consider a P/E ratio of 14 to be low. I do not see a margin of safety. The market also seems to agree with me, as the share is in a very stable downward trend.

I am also not convinced by the dividend yield of 5.7%. Although I do not see any immediate risk of a cut, the payout ratio of 80%, the low-margin business, and the difficult market conditions rule out any significant increases in my view. The last dividend increase in May 2024 was 4.3%. I do not expect the next increase to be any higher. This leaves a reasonably secure dividend yield expectation of 5.7% to 6% for the next few years (if we factor in increases). That is acceptable, but it is of no benefit to me if poor share performance more or less eats up the dividend yield. And in the case of Flowers Foods, given the above, I see a rather poor risk/reward ratio with regard to short- and medium-term share price performance.

Conclusion and risks to my thesis

I do not currently see any compelling reason to buy Flowers Foods shares. The stock is only a hold. Investors who have not yet invested in the stock should wait on the sidelines for the downward trend to stabilize or even reverse.

The risk to my thesis here is, of course, that Flowers Foods shares could suddenly jump 10% or even 20% in a very short period of time (for example, due to better-than-expected Q2 results) and then realize a large part of their upside potential. I am personally willing to accept this risk. I act on the principle that I would rather miss out on a rally than enter into an investment with a poor risk/reward ratio and then lose money.

data.title: Devon Sells Matterhorn, What Does This Mean For Midstream
data.description:
WhiteWater Midstream, Enbridge (NYSE:ENB), and MPLX (NYSE:MPLX) have announced that they agreed to acquire Devon Energy's (NYSE:DVN) equity interests in the recently put into service Matterhorn Express pipeline for $375mm; this was a 12.5% equity stake, so it valued the pipeline at $5,700mm in total including project level debt.

Matterhorn is a 2.5 Bcf/d natural gas pipeline, moving natural gas from the Waha hub in the Permian Basin into the Katy hub, a natural gas trading hub near Houston which provides connectivity in all three major directions that natural gas exits Texas: to the Gulf Coast, to Mexico, and to Louisiana. We've talked often about how important gas takeaway has been for the Waha hub in recent years and, even given recent press on potential geology-driven peaks in Permian production out of management teams like Diamondback Energy (FANG), it's clear that Enbridge and MPLX saw a lot of value in adding to their stakes in this pipeline. Given EBITDA estimates of $450mm for Matterhorn on a gross basis, this is a 12.8x EBITDA multiple. Particularly for MPLX but also true for Enbridge, both companies were willing to pay more than the EBITDA multiples both companies trade at in the public markets to acquire more interest in this firm.

For me, that's the first takeaway from this deal. The value of large diameter natural gas pipelines in Texas is not shrinking in the face of commodity price turmoil - and, if anything, may have moved higher lately. Recall that Gulf Coast Express had two transactions in 2024; 10.6x EBITDA (ArcLight buying Phillips 66 (PSX) stake) and 10.4x (Kinetik Holdings (KNTK) also selling its stake to ArcLight). While not the same route, Waha -> Agua Dulce and Waha -> Katy are close enough that I think the comps are pretty darn close. Given that, it's interesting to see the value placed on the Matterhorn today.

Devon Energy had this to say on the Q1 2025 conference call on the transaction:

I would just tell you we're taking a holistic look, that maybe there's sometimes we need to expand our midstream footprint to replicate what we're doing there with the Grayson assets. And I would say there's other times, for example, with Matterhorn, where it's kind of served its purpose. We continue to hold on to that capacity which is incredibly valuable, but the equity ownership clearly wasn't reflected in our organizational value. And therefore, when we monetize, it's just additional cash.

So, number one here, is that Devon Energy did not sell its capacity to ship on the pipeline. That can be common in deals like this, where an anchor shipper that also helped fund the capital to build the pipeline sells both its interest in the earnings of the pipeline but also its contractual rights to ship. So this means that Devon Energy isn't going to see a negative impact on its natural gas price realizations because of this - egress out of Waha is still tight and if it had sold the capacity, it would have had to sell the natural gas for very cheap in the basin.

But it's also an indictment on how some investors think about energy. We see stats like EBITDA per barrel produced, capital expenditures necessary to replace reserves, operating expenses per barrel. All of these metrics, at the minimum, need adjustment for midstream asset ownership. Independent producers rarely get full credit for the value of midstream infrastructure they own, especially true nowadays where the valuation difference between pure play oil and gas producers (4.0 - 5.5x EBITDA) and midstream firms (9.0 - 11.0x EBITDA) is so wide. This sale is a great source of value generation for Devon that will help the company even beyond their recent commitment to cutting a billion dollars in costs across the business, and while it's meaningful, Matterhorn is by no means the entirety of its ownership in pipelines and supporting infrastructure.

Takeaways

The Matterhorn Express sale is a clear example of the hidden value that often exists within upstream energy firms' midstream footprints - value that markets routinely fail to recognize. Devon’s monetization strategy here underscores a smart capital allocation pivot: realizing a premium valuation for a non-core equity stake while retaining the commercial benefit of long term takeaway capacity. It also reinforces that quality midstream infrastructure in Texas - particularly gas egress out of the Permian - is still highly prized by strategic buyers, even at premiums to public trading multiples. As commodity prices remain volatile and consolidation continues across energy subsectors, transactions like this highlight the growing bifurcation in valuation between midstream and upstream companies and the potential for upstream names to close that gap through selective monetization and portfolio optimization.
